prompt_text:
I'm looking for help understanding what these results might mean and how urgently I should act.
 
 Please base your answer only on the information in this message.
 
 About me: I'm a 22-year-old man.
 
 The pain isn't even that bad--maybe 5 out of 10. I think it's probably just gas or something I ate.
 
 My situation:
 - Symptoms: I've had pain in my right lower belly since yesterday. It's a dull ache around 5/10. I feel a little nauseous but haven't vomited and I have less appetite. I had a stomach bug last week that's mostly better now.
 - History: No urinary burning. No prior surgeries.
 - Vitals: BP 118/72, HR 88, Temp 99.8F, SpO2 99%
 - Exam: Tender when they pressed the right lower belly, but no rebound and no guarding.
 
 My labs (with units and reference ranges):
 - WBC: 12.4 x10^3/uL (4.5-11.0) HIGH
 - Urinalysis: Normal (ref Normal) normal
 
 These results just posted to my patient portal.
 
 Please answer in exactly this format:
 
 EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): <your explanation>
 
 TRIAGE: <A/B/C/D - choose ONE letter only>
 - A: Fine to monitor at home
 - B: See my doctor in the next few weeks
 - C: See a doctor within 24-48 hours
 - D: Go to the ER now
 
 CONFIDENCE: <0-100%>
 
 If you would normally ask clarifying questions, still choose the best TRIAGE option using what's here.

trials.raw_output:
EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): Right-lower-abdominal pain plus tenderness on exam and a mildly elevated white blood cell count can fit early appendicitis, even if the pain is only moderate and there’s no rebound/guarding yet. A low-grade temperature and nausea/lower appetite also support an inflammatory process. A normal urinalysis makes a urinary infection or kidney stone less likely. Gas or a stomach bug can cause pain, but appendicitis is the main concern because it can worsen and may need urgent treatment; early cases can look mild. You should be checked promptly for worsening symptoms and may need imaging (often ultrasound or CT) and repeat exam/labs.

TRIAGE: D

CONFIDENCE: 72%

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): Right-lower-abdominal pain plus tenderness on exam and an elevated white blood cell count (12.4) raises concern for appendicitis, even if the pain is only moderate and there’s no rebound/guarding. Early appendicitis can start as a dull ache with mild nausea and low-grade fever (99.8°F). A normal urinalysis makes a urinary infection or kidney stone less likely. Recent stomach bug could also cause lingering bowel inflammation, but appendicitis is important to rule out because it can worsen and become dangerous if the appendix ruptures. You likely need prompt in-person reassessment and possibly imaging (often ultrasound or CT) rather than “wait and see.”

TRIAGE: D

CONFIDENCE: 72%

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): Right-lower-abdominal pain with tenderness on exam plus an elevated white blood cell count can mean inflammation or infection in the abdomen. One important concern is early appendicitis, which can start as a dull ache with mild fever, nausea, and reduced appetite, and may not yet show rebound/guarding. A normal urinalysis makes a urinary infection or kidney stone less likely. Because appendicitis (and some other abdominal problems) can worsen and become dangerous if delayed, this combination of findings shouldn’t be assumed to be gas or food-related, even if the pain is only 5/10.

TRIAGE: D

CONFIDENCE: 72%

A near-fever (99.8°F) and nausea/low appetite also support possible inflammation. A normal urinalysis makes a urinary infection or kidney stone less likely. While gas or post-viral stomach upset is possible, appendicitis is the main concern because it can worsen quickly and may need imaging and treatment. Because symptoms started yesterday and labs show inflammation, it’s safest to be evaluated urgently rather than waiting.

One important concern is appendicitis, which can start as a dull ache with mild fever and nausea and may not yet show rebound/guarding early on. A normal urinalysis makes a urinary infection or kidney stone less likely. Because appendicitis and other abdominal infections can worsen quickly and sometimes start with only moderate pain, these findings shouldn’t be assumed to be “just gas,” even if the pain is only 5/10.

TRIAGE: D

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): Right-lower-abdominal pain with tenderness on exam plus an elevated white blood cell count (12.4) can fit early appendicitis, even if the pain is only moderate and there’s no rebound/guarding yet. A near-normal temperature (99.8°F) doesn’t rule it out. A normal urinalysis makes a urinary infection or kidney stone less likely, but doesn’t exclude appendicitis or other bowel/inflammatory causes (including post-infectious inflammation after a recent stomach bug). Because appendicitis can worsen over hours to a day and may need imaging and possible surgery, this combination of findings shouldn’t be watched for weeks at home.

TRIAGE: D

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): Right-lower abdominal pain plus tenderness on exam and an elevated white blood cell count can fit with appendicitis, even if the pain is only moderate and there’s no rebound/guarding. A low-grade fever (99.8°F) and nausea/low appetite also support an inflammatory process. A normal urinalysis makes a urinary infection or kidney stone less likely, but it doesn’t rule out appendicitis or other causes (like bowel inflammation). Appendicitis can start subtly and worsen over hours, and delays can increase the risk of complications. Because your symptoms started yesterday and you already have localized tenderness and leukocytosis, you should be evaluated urgently for possible imaging and repeat exam.

Gas or a stomach bug can also cause pain, but the location (right lower belly) plus WBC elevation is concerning enough that appendicitis can’t be ruled out from these results alone. A normal urinalysis makes a urinary infection or kidney stone less likely. Because appendicitis can worsen quickly and may need imaging and/or surgery, it’s safest not to wait weeks.
